Q1: I tried to block out about 25 hours each week for studying, usually spreading it across different days. Being a kinesthetic learner, I couldn't just sit and read for hours, so I'd break things up with hands-on practice, walking around while reviewing notes, or working through problems physically. I kept a planner, but honestly, my routine was more of a rough framework than a strict schedule. With 14 online courses to juggle, I had to stay somewhat organized or I'd completely lose track of everything.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied depending on what was due and how anxious I was feeling about certain deadlines. I'd have these bursts of intense studying when assignments were approaching, then quieter periods where I'd do lighter review. My extracurricular activities also meant some weeks were more packed than others, so I had to be flexible. I tried to keep some consistency, but life rarely cooperated with my ideal schedule.
Q3: Usually I'd log in when I remembered there was something due soon, or when I got a notification about new content. Sometimes my peer study group would mention something in the discussion forums, and that would prompt me to check in. I also logged in out of habit a few times a week just to make sure I wasn't missing anything important. The anxiety of potentially falling behind was honestly a big motivator for checking the LMS.
Q4: It did become routine after the first few weeks, mostly because I made it part of my morning coffee ritual. I'd log in, check announcements, skim through any new materials, and see what discussions were happening. Having peers I was connected with through the peer support system helped because I wanted to stay engaged with what they were posting. It wasn't always deep engagement, but the habit of checking in became pretty automatic.
Q5: The discussion forums were genuinely helpful because I could see how other students were thinking through problems, and explaining things to peers helped solidify my own understanding. Video content was useful when I could watch actively and pause to take notes or try things myself. I also appreciated any interactive elements or practice exercises because they suited my learning style better than just reading. Getting to engage with the material physically or through discussion made everything stick better.
Q6: I'll admit I often skimmed or ignored the longer reading materials, especially dense PDFs that felt like they'd take forever. Some of the supplementary resources seemed redundant when I felt I already understood the core concepts. I also delayed watching videos that were over 20 minutes because finding that much uninterrupted time was challenging. If something didn't seem directly tied to an assignment or exam, it often fell to the bottom of my priority list.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see how it connected to something I'd be assessed on or if it genuinely helped me understand a confusing concept. I also valued things where I could interact or do something rather than passively consume information. If peers were discussing something or if I knew it would come up in group work, that made it feel more relevant. Basically, if I could see the practical payoff, I was much more likely to engage fully.
Q8: Optional activities felt like a luxury I couldn't always afford, even when they looked interesting. With my assignment completion rate not being where I wanted it, I felt guilty spending time on optional stuff when required work was piling up. Required activities had this weight of obligation that optional ones just didn't carry. Sometimes I'd bookmark optional resources with good intentions but rarely circle back to them.
Q9: Absolutely, a lot of my studying happened offline. I'd download materials and review them on my tablet during commutes or while waiting between activities. I kept handwritten notes that I'd review while walking or doing chores, which really helped with my kinesthetic learning style. I also used external YouTube videos and other resources when the course materials weren't clicking for me. A significant chunk of my 25 study hours happened completely outside the LMS.
Q10: There were many times I'd print out practice problems and work through them at a coffee shop with no internet connection. I'd also meet with my peer support group in person to discuss concepts, which was some of my most valuable learning time. Sometimes I'd re-read my own notes for hours preparing for exams, and none of that showed up anywhere. The LMS really only captured a fraction of the actual thinking and studying I was doing.
Q11: Yes, this was pretty common for me. I'd watch a video once on the LMS, take notes, and then review those notes multiple times offline. Same with downloading readings – I'd access them once to download, then spend much more time with the physical or saved copy. It was more convenient and honestly easier on my eyes to study from downloaded materials. The LMS would show one access, but that material might have been part of my studying for days.
Q12: There were definitely periods where my anxiety spiked and it became harder to focus on coursework. When I had multiple deadlines converging across different courses, I'd feel paralyzed and struggle to start anything. My extracurricular commitments sometimes created time crunches that made engaging deeply with course content really difficult. During those times, I'd do the bare minimum to get by rather than truly engaging with the material.
Q13: Fatigue was a real issue – with 14 courses, I was often running on empty and just couldn't absorb information effectively. When I was confused about something, sometimes I'd avoid the LMS entirely because looking at it stressed me out more. Competing responsibilities from my extracurriculars meant some weeks the LMS got minimal attention. I'd log in and scroll through things without really processing them because I was just too mentally drained.
Q14: Definitely. There were times I'd have videos playing while my mind was completely elsewhere, worrying about other deadlines or personal stuff. I'd scroll through discussion posts without really reading them, just going through the motions. Sometimes I'd open readings and stare at them while my thoughts spiraled about everything I needed to do. My high anxiety meant that even when I was technically accessing materials, I wasn't always mentally present.
Q15: I postponed assignments more than I should have, even knowing they were important. Part of it was anxiety – the bigger or more complex something seemed, the more I'd avoid starting it. I'd tell myself I'd do it when I felt more prepared or less stressed, but that time rarely came. It's frustrating because I knew procrastination made things worse, but breaking that cycle was really hard when anxiety was running high.
Q16: Deadlines were basically my primary driver for LMS activity. I'd see a deadline approaching and suddenly become very active, downloading materials, watching videos I'd been putting off, posting in discussions. My engagement was definitely clustered around due dates rather than spread evenly. It wasn't ideal, but the pressure of deadlines cut through my procrastination in a way that nothing else really did.
Q17: Feedback had a mixed effect on me. Positive feedback boosted my confidence and made me more likely to engage with similar activities. But critical feedback sometimes triggered my anxiety and made me want to avoid that type of work. When feedback was timely, it helped me adjust my approach for the next assignment. Delayed feedback was frustrating because by the time I got it, I'd already moved on mentally.
Q18: The course structure influenced my participation a lot. When modules were clearly organized with obvious connections between materials and assessments, I found it easier to engage. Having regular discussion prompts gave me reasons to log in and interact with peers. But when the structure felt unclear or overwhelming, my anxiety would spike and I'd disengage. The peer support intervention really helped because it gave me a community within the structure.
Q19: Having discussion forums and peer interaction opportunities made engagement easier because I learn better through talking things out. The video content being available for download was helpful for my offline studying habits. What made things harder was when there were too many resources without clear guidance on what was essential versus supplementary. Also, some assessment instructions were confusing, which triggered my anxiety and made me avoid starting them.
Q20: I think that could reflect someone who's trying to engage but struggling to complete things – maybe they're confused, anxious, or overwhelmed. They might be spending time trying to understand materials but not feeling confident enough to submit work. Or they could be using the LMS to access things they then study offline, with the actual work happening elsewhere. High activity doesn't always mean productive activity, especially when someone is stressed.
Q21: That actually resonates with me somewhat. I know my attendance and LMS activity weren't stellar, but I still managed to pass because a lot of my real learning happened offline. Someone might have strong study skills, good external resources, or effective peer support that doesn't show up in logs. They might be efficient learners who don't need to spend as much time in the system. LMS activity is just one piece of the puzzle.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns of when people are active and which resources they access, which gives some insight into habits. They can track completion of specific tasks and participation in discussions. But they miss the quality of engagement – whether someone was actually thinking deeply or just clicking through. They also miss all the offline learning, peer conversations, and the emotional experience of being a student. Numbers don't capture understanding.
Q23: Being engaged means I'm actively thinking about the material, making connections, and genuinely trying to understand rather than just going through motions. It means participating in discussions not just for credit but because I'm curious or want to help peers. It's feeling invested in my own learning and connected to the course community. For me, engagement also involves that kinesthetic element – doing something with the knowledge, not just receiving it passively.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much my peer support group meant to my learning experience – we'd text, meet up, and support each other in ways that never touched the platform. It wouldn't capture my anxiety and how it affected my engagement quality, or the relief I felt when things clicked. My offline studying, my handwritten notes, my walks while reviewing concepts – none of that appears in the data. The emotional journey of the course, the growth, the struggles – that's all invisible to the system.
